fixSteps.title: Replace motor or caliper
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the EPB motor or complete caliper assembly. Bleed the brake system and perform EPB initialization with the scan tool.

faq.q: Should I replace both EPB motors at the same time?
faq.a: If one motor has failed from wear, the other is likely near the end of its life too. Replacing both is a good practice, especially since the labor is similar.

faq.q: How do I retract the EPB pistons for a brake job?
faq.a: You must use a scan tool to enter EPB service mode, which retracts the caliper pistons. Never try to push them back manually as this can damage the motor mechanism.

title: Electronic Parking Brake Switch Circuit
shortDescription: Fault in the electronic parking brake control switch circuit on the dashboard.
fullDescription:
Code C0615 indicates a malfunction in the electronic parking brake switch circuit. The EPB switch is the button or toggle on the dashboard or center console that the driver uses to apply and release the parking brake. The switch sends a signal to the EPB module, which then commands the rear caliper motors.

Switch circuit faults can include the switch itself failing (worn contacts, stuck button), a wiring problem between the switch and EPB module, or a module input circuit failure. Some EPB switches also include a force sensor that detects how hard the driver pulls or pushes the switch.

A failed EPB switch can leave the parking brake in whatever state it was in when the switch failed. If the brake was applied, it may remain locked. If released, the driver cannot apply the parking brake using the switch. Most vehicles have an alternative method to apply or release the EPB in case of switch failure.
safetyImpact: Driver cannot manually control the parking brake via the dashboard switch. Automatic parking brake features may still function on some vehicles.

causes.cause: Wiring fault between switch and module

symptoms: Cannot apply or release parking brake manually
EPB auto-hold may still work on some vehicles

fixSteps.title: Test switch signal
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to monitor the EPB switch input signal while pressing the switch. If the module does not see a signal change, the switch or wiring is faulty.

fixSteps.title: Inspect switch
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the trim around the EPB switch and inspect the connector. Check for spilled drinks, corrosion, or loose connections.

fixSteps.title: Test switch electrically
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the switch and test it with a multimeter. The switch should show continuity change when actuated.

fixSteps.title: Replace switch
fixSteps.instruction: If the switch has failed, replace it. Most EPB switches are a simple plug-in replacement. Clear codes and test operation.

faq.q: Can I still park safely without the EPB switch working?
faq.a: Use the transmission park position (automatic) or leave the vehicle in gear (manual). On steep hills, use wheel chocks. Get the switch repaired promptly.

faq.q: Will automatic parking brake features still work?
faq.a: On some vehicles, the automatic apply/release function (triggered by shifting to Park or opening the door) operates independently of the manual switch and may continue working.

title: TPMS System Not Responding
shortDescription: The TPMS control module is not responding to sensor transmissions or system queries.
fullDescription:
Code C0700 indicates that the Tire Pressure Monitoring System is completely non-responsive. The TPMS module or receiver is not communicating with the vehicle's network or is not receiving any signals from the tire pressure sensors. This represents a total system failure rather than an individual sensor issue.

TPMS systems use radio frequency (RF) signals transmitted from sensors inside each tire to a receiver module in the vehicle. The receiver processes these signals and provides pressure data to the instrument cluster. A complete system failure can be caused by a dead receiver module, total loss of power to the TPMS system, or a vehicle network communication fault.

This code is distinct from individual sensor faults (which would produce sensor-specific codes) because no sensor communication is occurring at all.
safetyImpact: No automatic tire pressure monitoring. Driver must manually check tire pressures regularly. No warning for rapid pressure loss.

causes.cause: TPMS module power supply fault (fuse or wiring)
CAN bus communication fault isolating TPMS module

symptoms: No tire pressure readings on dashboard
TPMS indicator may flash then stay solid
All tire pressure values show as unavailable

fixSteps.title: Check TPMS fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the TPMS fuse in the fuse box and test it. A blown fuse will cause total TPMS system failure.

fixSteps.title: Scan for communication codes
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a full system scan looking for U-codes (communication faults) related to the TPMS module. A U0300 or similar code would indicate a network communication issue.
tools: OBD-II scanner with full system scan

fixSteps.title: Check TPMS module
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the TPMS module and check for power and ground at its connector. Verify the module is communicating on the CAN bus.

fixSteps.title: Replace TPMS module
fixSteps.instruction: If the module has failed, replace it. The new module may require programming with the vehicle's tire sensor IDs.
tools: TPMS tool for sensor ID programming

faq.q: Is it legal to drive without TPMS?
faq.a: In most jurisdictions, there is no law requiring TPMS to be functional after purchase. However, it is a safety system, and a vehicle with a TPMS fault may fail inspection in some states.

faq.q: Can I still check tire pressure manually?
faq.a: Absolutely. Use a quality handheld tire pressure gauge to check all four tires (and the spare) at least monthly and before long trips. This is good practice even with functioning TPMS.

title: TPMS Sensor Battery Low – Left Front
shortDescription: The left front TPMS sensor has a low battery and may stop transmitting.
fullDescription:
Code C0710 indicates that the left front tire pressure sensor's internal battery is depleted or nearing the end of its life. TPMS sensors contain a small lithium battery with a typical lifespan of 5-10 years. The battery is not replaceable — when it dies, the entire sensor must be replaced.

The sensor transmits a low-battery flag as part of its regular data transmission, alerting the system that the sensor will soon stop working. This is a proactive warning that allows the driver to plan sensor replacement before complete failure.

When the battery dies completely, the TPMS system will lose communication with that sensor and display a TPMS malfunction warning. Since TPMS sensor batteries all have similar lifespans, a low battery in one sensor often means the others will follow within months.
safetyImpact: TPMS coverage for the left front tire will be lost soon. No immediate safety concern if tire pressure is manually monitored.

causes.cause: Normal battery depletion from age (5-10 years)
Excessive sensor transmissions due to system fault

symptoms: TPMS light may flash then stay solid
Intermittent loss of left front pressure reading
Sensor may stop transmitting in cold weather first

fixSteps.title: Confirm sensor age
fixSteps.instruction: Check when the TPMS sensors were last replaced. If they are 5+ years old, battery depletion is expected.

fixSteps.title: Plan replacement
fixSteps.instruction: Schedule tire service for sensor replacement. It is most cost-effective to replace sensors during a tire change since the tire must be dismounted to access the sensor.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Dismount the left front tire, replace the TPMS sensor on the wheel rim, remount and balance the tire. Program the new sensor ID to the vehicle.

fixSteps.title: Consider replacing all sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Since all sensors are the same age, consider replacing all four at once to avoid repeated tire dismounting. Bundle with tire replacement if due.

faq.q: Can the TPMS sensor battery be replaced?
faq.a: No. The battery is sealed inside the sensor and cannot be replaced. The entire sensor must be replaced. Aftermarket sensors are available at lower cost than OEM.

faq.q: Should I replace all four sensors at once?
faq.a: It is recommended because they all have similar battery life. Replacing all four during a tire change saves labor costs compared to doing them one at a time.

title: TPMS Sensor Battery Low – Right Front
shortDescription: The right front TPMS sensor has a low battery and will need replacement.
fullDescription:
Code C0715 indicates that the right front tire pressure monitoring sensor has a depleted or near-depleted internal battery. Like all TPMS sensors, the battery is non-replaceable and sealed within the sensor body to withstand the harsh environment inside a tire.

TPMS sensors transmit pressure and temperature data at regular intervals, typically every 30-60 seconds while driving and less frequently when the vehicle is stationary. Each transmission uses battery power, and the cumulative transmissions over years of use eventually exhaust the battery.

This code serves as an advance warning that the sensor will soon stop transmitting. Cold weather often accelerates the final failure because low temperatures reduce battery output voltage below the sensor's minimum operating threshold.
safetyImpact: TPMS monitoring for the right front tire will be lost when the battery dies completely.
symptoms: Right front pressure reading intermittent or absent
More likely to lose signal in cold weather

fixSteps.title: Verify sensor status
fixSteps.instruction: Use a TPMS activation tool to trigger the right front sensor and check its battery status report.

fixSteps.title: Schedule replacement
fixSteps.instruction: Plan to replace the sensor during the next tire service. Coordinate with tire rotation or replacement if upcoming.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Dismount the tire, install a new TPMS sensor, remount and balance. Register the new sensor with the vehicle's TPMS system.

faq.q: How long will the sensor work after a low battery warning?
faq.a: It varies. The sensor may continue to function for weeks or months after the initial low battery flag, but it will eventually stop transmitting, typically failing in cold weather first.

faq.q: Can I use aftermarket TPMS sensors?
faq.a: Yes. Aftermarket and universal TPMS sensors are available at lower cost than OEM. They must be programmed to match the vehicle's sensor protocol.

title: TPMS Sensor Signal Not Received – Left Rear
shortDescription: The TPMS module is not receiving any signal from the left rear tire pressure sensor.
fullDescription:
Code C0750 is set when the TPMS receiver has not received a transmission from the left rear tire pressure sensor within the expected time window. Unlike a low-battery code, this indicates complete loss of communication — the sensor may have a dead battery, be physically damaged, or have been removed.

This code is commonly triggered after tire service if the sensor was damaged during tire dismounting, if the tire was replaced and the sensor was not transferred, or if aftermarket wheels were installed without TPMS sensors. It can also occur if the sensor has completely failed or if its battery has fully discharged.

The TPMS system typically allows a time window of several minutes to an hour of driving before flagging a missing sensor. This prevents false codes during tire changes or sensor relearn procedures.
safetyImpact: No TPMS coverage for the left rear tire. The driver will not receive automatic warning of low tire pressure on that wheel.

causes.cause: Sensor not installed (aftermarket wheels or missing)
Sensor ID not programmed to vehicle

symptoms: Left rear tire pressure not displayed
TPMS malfunction indicator flashing then solid

fixSteps.title: Activate sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Use a TPMS activation tool to trigger the left rear sensor. If no response, the sensor is dead or missing.

fixSteps.title: Verify sensor presence
fixSteps.instruction: If recently serviced, confirm the sensor was reinstalled. Check if the tire was mounted on a wheel with no sensor.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor if needed
fixSteps.instruction: Dismount the tire, install a new TPMS sensor, and remount. Program the new sensor ID to the vehicle.

fixSteps.title: Perform TPMS relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Follow the vehicle's TPMS relearn procedure to register all four sensor IDs with the TPMS module.

faq.q: Did the tire shop break my sensor?
faq.a: It is possible. TPMS sensors can be damaged during tire dismounting if the technician is not careful. Reputable shops check sensors before and after service.

faq.q: Do I need a relearn procedure after replacing a sensor?
faq.a: Yes. The new sensor has a unique ID that must be programmed into the vehicle's TPMS module. The procedure varies by manufacturer — some require a scan tool, others use a specific driving pattern.

title: ABS Hydraulic Pump Motor Control Circuit – Manufacturer Specific
shortDescription: Manufacturer-specific fault in the ABS hydraulic pump motor control electronics.
fullDescription:
Code C1001 is a manufacturer-specific chassis code that indicates a fault in the ABS hydraulic pump motor control circuit. While the exact definition varies by manufacturer, it commonly refers to a problem with the electronic driver circuit within the ABS module that controls the pump motor.

This code is distinct from generic pump motor codes because it specifically addresses the electronic control side rather than the motor itself. The ABS module uses power transistors (MOSFETs or IGBTs) to switch the pump motor on and off. When these transistors fail or their monitoring circuits detect an abnormality, this manufacturer-specific code is set.

Because this is a manufacturer-specific code, the exact diagnostic procedure and repair may vary. Consult the manufacturer's service manual for vehicle-specific information.
safetyImpact: ABS pump cannot be electronically controlled. All ABS, traction control, and stability control functions are disabled.

causes.cause: ABS module internal power transistor failure

symptoms: ABS pump does not operate during braking events
Multiple manufacturer-specific codes may be present

fixSteps.title: Check TSBs
fixSteps.instruction: Search for manufacturer Technical Service Bulletins related to this code for your specific vehicle. Some manufacturers have issued recalls or extended warranties for ABS module failures.

fixSteps.title: Verify power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Check the ABS module power supply voltage and ground circuit for voltage drops. High resistance in either circuit can trigger internal protection codes.

fixSteps.title: Check for thermal issues
fixSteps.instruction: If the code appears after extended driving, the module may be overheating. Verify mounting hardware and heat dissipation path are intact.

fixSteps.title: Replace or rebuild module
fixSteps.instruction: If the internal driver circuit has failed, the ABS module needs replacement or professional rebuild. Check for manufacturer warranty coverage.

faq.q: Is this code covered under a recall?
faq.a: Some manufacturers have issued recalls or extended warranty coverage for specific ABS module failures. Check the NHTSA recall database with your VIN for applicable campaigns.

faq.q: What does manufacturer-specific mean?
faq.a: Codes starting with C1 are defined by the vehicle manufacturer rather than a universal standard. The same code number may mean different things on different vehicle brands.

title: ABS Module Power Relay Feedback Circuit
shortDescription: The ABS module is not receiving the expected feedback signal from its main power relay.
fullDescription:
Code C1010 is a manufacturer-specific code indicating that the ABS module is not detecting the expected feedback signal from the main ABS power relay. The ABS module uses a feedback circuit to confirm that the relay has actually switched on or off as commanded. When the feedback does not match the command, this code is set.

The feedback circuit serves as a safety check — if the module commands the relay off but feedback shows it is still on, the system knows there is a stuck relay that could cause unintended ABS operation. Conversely, if the module commands the relay on but no feedback is received, the system knows power is not reaching the ABS components.

This code typically results in complete ABS system shutdown because the module cannot confirm that its power supply is controllable.
safetyImpact: ABS system is disabled due to inability to verify power relay operation.

causes.cause: ABS module internal feedback circuit failure

symptoms: Relay may click normally but system stays disabled

fixSteps.title: Test relay
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the ABS power relay and swap it with a known good identical relay to test. Clear codes and check if the system initializes.

fixSteps.title: Check relay socket
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the relay socket for corrosion, burned terminals, or loose connections. Pay attention to the feedback wire terminal.

fixSteps.title: Test feedback circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Using the wiring diagram, identify the feedback wire and test for continuity between the relay socket and the ABS module connector.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty relay, repair any damaged wiring, or if the module's internal feedback circuit has failed, replace the module.

faq.q: What is a relay feedback circuit?
faq.a: It is an additional wire from the relay's output terminal back to the control module. It allows the module to confirm that the relay actually switched and power is flowing as expected.

faq.q: Can a $10 relay fix this code?
faq.a: Often yes. The relay is the most common cause and is the least expensive component in the circuit. Always try relay replacement first before more expensive diagnosis.

title: ABS Wheel Speed Sensor Erratic – All Wheels (Manufacturer Specific)
shortDescription: All wheel speed sensor signals are intermittently erratic according to manufacturer-specific diagnostic criteria.
fullDescription:
Code C1035 is a manufacturer-specific version of the all-wheels-erratic code, typically with tighter diagnostic thresholds than the generic C0075. Different manufacturers set different sensitivity levels for detecting erratic sensor behavior, and this code uses the manufacturer's proprietary algorithm.

This code often appears on vehicles where the ABS module firmware is more sensitive to voltage fluctuations that affect sensor signal quality. It can be triggered by marginal battery or charging system performance that would not cause generic codes.

Some manufacturers have issued Technical Service Bulletins for this code, often pointing to specific electrical causes or providing updated module calibrations that reduce the sensitivity to normal electrical noise.
safetyImpact: ABS and stability control are disabled. All advanced braking safety systems are inoperative.

causes.cause: Charging system not meeting manufacturer's voltage specification
ABS module sensitivity to electrical noise
Battery terminal corrosion causing voltage drops

symptoms: All wheel speed readings appear erratic on scan tool

fixSteps.title: Check for TSBs
fixSteps.instruction: Search for Technical Service Bulletins for your specific vehicle related to this code. Some manufacturers have issued software updates that resolve false triggering.

fixSteps.title: Test charging system
fixSteps.instruction: Measure battery voltage at the battery terminals and at the ABS module connector. Check alternator output and look for voltage drops in the main power feed.

fixSteps.title: Clean battery connections
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean battery terminals and ground connections. Corrosion causes voltage drops that can affect sensitive ABS electronics.

fixSteps.title: Update module software
fixSteps.instruction: If a TSB exists with a software update, have the ABS module reprogrammed at a dealer or qualified shop with manufacturer-level scan tool access.

faq.q: What is a TSB?
faq.a: A Technical Service Bulletin is a document issued by the vehicle manufacturer to dealerships describing a known problem and its fix. TSBs are not recalls but acknowledge common issues.

faq.q: Can cleaning battery terminals really fix an ABS code?
faq.a: Yes. The ABS module is sensitive to supply voltage quality. Even slight voltage drops from corroded battery terminals can cause the module to misinterpret sensor signals.

title: ABS Hydraulic Pump Motor Stalled
shortDescription: The ABS hydraulic pump motor has stalled or is not reaching the required speed.
fullDescription:
Code C1095 is a manufacturer-specific code set when the ABS module detects that the hydraulic pump motor has stalled during operation or is not reaching its required rotational speed. The ABS pump must spin at a specific speed to generate adequate hydraulic pressure for ABS, traction control, and stability control functions.

A stalled motor can be caused by mechanical seizure from internal corrosion, a failed motor winding, inadequate power supply, or contaminated brake fluid that has caused the pump's internal valves to seize. The module monitors motor current and speed (via back-EMF or a dedicated speed sensor) to detect this condition.

Repeated stall events can cause the motor to overheat, potentially leading to permanent damage if the root cause is not addressed.
safetyImpact: ABS cannot generate the hydraulic pressure needed for anti-lock braking, traction control, or stability control.

causes.cause: Contaminated brake fluid causing internal valve seizure
Low voltage preventing motor from reaching speed

symptoms: Grinding or buzzing noise from ABS pump area

fixSteps.title: Check battery and power
fixSteps.instruction: Measure battery voltage and the voltage at the ABS pump motor connector during motor operation. Low voltage can prevent the motor from spinning.

fixSteps.title: Check brake fluid condition
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect brake fluid in the reservoir. Dark, cloudy, or gritty fluid indicates contamination that can damage the pump internals.

fixSteps.title: Test motor directly
fixSteps.instruction: With adequate voltage applied directly to the motor, listen for smooth operation. A stalling or grinding motor indicates internal damage.

fixSteps.title: Replace ABS unit
fixSteps.instruction: A stalled pump motor typically requires replacement of the ABS hydraulic unit. Flush the brake system with clean fluid during installation.

faq.q: Can contaminated brake fluid really damage the ABS pump?
faq.a: Yes. Moisture-contaminated brake fluid causes internal corrosion, and degraded fluid can deposit varnish on internal valve surfaces, both of which can seize the pump mechanism.

faq.q: Is it worth rebuilding versus replacing the ABS unit?
faq.a: Rebuilding can save 40-60% of the cost of a new unit. Reputable ABS rebuilders replace worn components and test the unit to manufacturer specifications.

title: Engine Torque Signal Fault (Manufacturer Specific)
shortDescription: The ABS/ESC module is not receiving a valid engine torque signal from the engine control module.
fullDescription:
Code C1130 is set when the ABS/ESC module detects that the engine torque signal received from the engine control module (ECM) via the CAN bus is missing, invalid, or not updating as expected. The stability control system uses engine torque data to coordinate traction control interventions — it can request the ECM to reduce engine torque when wheel slip is detected.

Without a valid torque signal, the traction control system cannot properly manage wheel spin through engine torque reduction. The system may still apply individual brakes to control wheel spin, but this is less effective and less refined than the combined approach.

This code often indicates a CAN bus communication issue between the engine control module and the ABS module rather than a problem with either module individually.
safetyImpact: Traction control effectiveness is reduced because engine torque cannot be modulated. ABS function may be unaffected.

causes.cause: CAN bus wiring fault between ECM and ABS module
ABS module CAN bus receiver fault
ECM software issue not broadcasting torque data

fixSteps.title: Scan all modules
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a full vehicle scan looking for U-codes in the ABS and engine modules that would indicate communication faults.
tools: OBD-II scanner with full system scan

fixSteps.title: Check CAN bus network
fixSteps.instruction: Measure CAN bus termination resistance at the OBD-II port (should be approximately 60 ohms). Check for proper CAN-H and CAN-L voltage levels.

fixSteps.title: Check engine module
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the engine control module is functioning and broadcasting data on the CAN bus. Check for engine codes that might indicate an ECM fault.

fixSteps.title: Repair communication fault
fixSteps.instruction: If a wiring fault is found, repair the CAN bus wires. If a module is faulty, replace and program it.

faq.q: Can a bad ground connection cause CAN bus issues?
faq.a: Yes. Module ground connections are critical for CAN bus communication. A poor ground can cause intermittent communication errors that affect multiple systems.

faq.q: Will traction control still work at all?
faq.a: Partially. The system can still apply individual wheel brakes to reduce spin, but it cannot request the engine to reduce power. This makes the intervention more abrupt and less effective.

title: Electric Power Steering Motor Circuit – Manufacturer Specific
shortDescription: A fault has been detected in the electric power steering (EPS) motor or its control circuit.
fullDescription:
Code C1145 is a manufacturer-specific code indicating a malfunction in the electric power steering motor circuit. Modern electric power steering systems use an electric motor mounted on or near the steering rack to provide steering assist. The motor is controlled by the EPS control module, which adjusts assist level based on vehicle speed, steering angle, and torque input.

Motor circuit faults can include open windings, short circuits, excessive current draw, or failure of the motor's internal position sensor. The EPS module continuously monitors motor health and will set this code when any abnormality is detected.

Loss of power steering assist makes the vehicle significantly harder to steer, especially at low speeds and during parking maneuvers. At highway speeds, the effect is less noticeable because the tires provide self-centering force.
safetyImpact: Power steering assist may be lost or reduced, making the vehicle difficult to steer, especially at low speeds. Emergency maneuvers become much harder.

causes.cause: EPS control module driver circuit failure

symptoms: Heavy/stiff steering especially at low speeds
Whining or grinding noise from steering column or rack

fixSteps.title: Check power supply
fixSteps.instruction: EPS systems require significant electrical power. Check battery voltage and the EPS fuse. A weak battery is a common cause of EPS faults.

fixSteps.title: Inspect motor connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the EPS motor (on the steering column or rack) and inspect its connector for corrosion, water intrusion, or damage.

fixSteps.title: Test motor resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the EPS motor winding resistance at the motor connector. Compare to manufacturer specifications. Infinite or zero resistance indicates motor failure.

fixSteps.title: Replace EPS motor or rack
fixSteps.instruction: If the motor has failed, replace the motor assembly or the entire steering rack depending on the vehicle design. Some vehicles require EPS calibration after replacement.

faq.q: Can I still drive without power steering?
faq.a: Technically yes, but the steering will be very heavy at low speeds. Highway driving is more manageable. Use caution, especially in parking lots and during emergency maneuvers.

faq.q: Can a weak battery cause power steering problems?
faq.a: Yes. Electric power steering draws significant current. A weak battery or failing alternator may not provide enough power, causing the EPS module to reduce or disable assist.

title: Electric Power Steering Torque Sensor Circuit
shortDescription: Fault detected in the EPS steering torque sensor that measures driver input force.
fullDescription:
Code C1155 indicates a malfunction in the electric power steering torque sensor circuit. The torque sensor is mounted in the steering column and measures how much force the driver is applying to the steering wheel. The EPS control module uses this data to determine how much assist to provide — more driver effort results in more motor assist.

The torque sensor is a precision device that produces a voltage signal proportional to the torsional twist in a section of the steering column. It must be extremely accurate because the EPS system relies on it as the primary input for assist calculation.

A faulty torque sensor can cause the EPS system to provide incorrect assist levels — potentially too much assist (making the steering feel disconnected and dangerous) or too little (making the steering heavy). For this reason, the EPS module typically disables assist entirely when a torque sensor fault is detected rather than risk providing incorrect assist.
safetyImpact: Power steering assist is disabled for safety because the system cannot determine driver intent. Steering becomes manual.

causes.cause: Sensor connector pin damage or corrosion
Steering column intermediate shaft play affecting sensor
EPS module torque sensor input circuit failure

symptoms: Complete loss of power steering assist
Steering may feel jerky or inconsistent before failing

fixSteps.title: Read sensor data
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor the torque sensor voltage on the scan tool while slowly turning the wheel. The signal should change smoothly and proportionally. Dead spots or jumps indicate sensor failure.

fixSteps.title: Check sensor connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the torque sensor connector on the steering column for corrosion, loose pins, or water intrusion.

fixSteps.title: Check for steering column play
fixSteps.instruction: Rock the steering wheel back and forth with the engine off. Any free play or clunking in the column can cause erratic torque sensor readings.

fixSteps.title: Replace torque sensor
fixSteps.instruction: The torque sensor is typically integrated into the steering column assembly. Replacement may require the entire column or a sub-assembly. EPS calibration is needed after replacement.

faq.q: Why does the system disable assist entirely instead of guessing?
faq.a: Providing incorrect assist is more dangerous than no assist. If the system provided maximum assist when the driver was barely touching the wheel, it could cause a loss-of-control accident.

faq.q: Is the torque sensor the same as the steering angle sensor?
faq.a: No. The torque sensor measures how much force you are applying. The steering angle sensor measures how far you have turned the wheel. They are different sensors serving different functions.
